coredata.dc:title: Colorimetric detection of ATP by inhibiting the Peroxidase-like activity of carbon dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Na
Wen, Jiahui
Yi, Danyang
Wei, Zixuan
Long, Yijuan
Zheng, Huzhi
coredata.dc:description: Adenosine triphosphate (ATP) is the main energy currency for cells and an important biomolecule involved in cellular reactions, whose abnormal levels are closely related to physical disease, thus it is extremely important to establish a convenient, fast and simple ATP monitoring method. Toward this end, we developed a facile method for colorimetric detection of ATP on the basis of the inhibiting effect of ATP on the peroxidase-like activity of carbon dots (CDs). The detection principle of this method was utilizing the peroxidase-like activity of CDs, which catalyze the oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) by H2O2 to generate blue products. However, the introduction of ATP in the system can inhibit the generation of blue products, so ATP can be colorimetric detected. This method exhibited high sensitivity with a detection limit of 34 nM and a wide linear range (0.050–2.0 μM). The as-proposed colorimetric ATP sensor was capable of detecting ATP in real samples accurately.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Peroxidase-like activity
Colorimetric detection
ATP

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: ATP inhibiting the peroxidase-like activity of CDs.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (XDJK2020C033).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image (a), HRTEM images (b), size distribution(c) and FTIR spectrum of the CDs (d).

(a) The absorption spectra of different catalytic reaction systems: (1) TMB + H2O2, (2) CDs + TMB + H2O2, (3) CDs + ATP + TMB + H2O2, (4) ATP + TMB + H2O2. (b) The absorbance at 652 nm of the system in presence of different concentration of ATP. Experimental conditions: 40 mM acetate buffer solution, pH = 4.0; 1.7 mg/L CDs; 5.0 mM H2O2 and 0.80 mM TMB; incubated at 60℃ for 30 min. The error bars represent the standard deviation (n = 3).

The optimization of pH (a), temperature (b), H2O2 concentration (c) and incubation time (d). ΔA = A0 − A as a criterion was used to optimize the conditions, where A0 and A is the absorbance in the absence and presence of 0.50 μM ATP, respectively. The error bars denote the standard deviation (n = 3).
(a) Fluorescence spectra of CDs + TA + H2O2 + ATP (0, 0.50, 1.0 μM). (b) ESR spectra of •OH in the system of CDs + DMPO + H2O2, CDs + DMPO + H2O2 + ATP (0.50 μM), CDs + DMPO + H2O2 + ATP (1.0 μM).

(a) Absorption spectra of colorimetric determination system in the presence of different concentrations of ATP (0, 0.05, 0.10, 0.20, 0.40, 0.80, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0 μM). (b) calibration curve of ATP detection. (c) Visualization pictures related to linearity. Experimental conditions: 40 mM acetate buffer solution, pH = 4.0; 1.7 mg/L CDs; 5.0 mM H2O2 and 0.80 mM TMB; incubated at 60℃ for 30 min. The error bars represent the standard deviation (n = 3).

Selectivity for ATP detection. (a) Responses of the chromogenic system to different substances; (b) Effects of coexisting substances on the determination of 0.5 μM ATP. Experimental conditions: 40 mM acetate buffer solution, pH = 4.0; 1.7 mg/L CDs; 5.0 mM H2O2 and 0.80 mM TMB; incubated at 60℃ for 30 min. (0.10 μM for GTP, UTP, CTP, 0.50 μM for ADP, GDP, AMP and 5.0 μM for amino acids). The error bars represent the standard deviation (n = 3).
Schematic illustration of colorimetric detection of ATP.
Determination of ATP in FBS sample.

head.ce:title: Colorimetric detection of ATP by inhibiting the Peroxidase-like activity of carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Beibei, Chongqing 400715, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Beibei, Chongqing 400715, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Adenosine triphosphate (ATP) is the main energy currency for cells and an important biomolecule involved in cellular reactions, whose abnormal levels are closely related to physical disease, thus it is extremely important to establish a convenient, fast and simple ATP monitoring method. Toward this end, we developed a facile method for colorimetric detection of ATP on the basis of the inhibiting effect of ATP on the peroxidase-like activity of carbon dots (CDs). The detection principle of this method was utilizing the peroxidase-like activity of CDs, which catalyze the oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) by H2O2 to generate blue products. However, the introduction of ATP in the system can inhibit the generation of blue products, so ATP can be colorimetric detected. This method exhibited high sensitivity with a detection limit of 34 nM and a wide linear range (0.050–2.0 μM). The as-proposed colorimetric ATP sensor was capable of detecting ATP in real samples accurately.

ce:section.ce:para: Adenosine triphosphate (ATP), which is a major energy carrier in all living organisms, plays a significant role in many biological processes such as DNA synthesis, extracellular signaling mediator, hormonal and neuronal activities[1–3]. In addition, abnormal ATP levels may lead to a number of serious diseases, such as cardiovascular[4], Parkinson’s[5] and Alzheimer’s diseases[6], thus ATP is often used as an indicator for disease diagnosis. Therefore, the determination of ATP concentration in biological samples is very important, so researchers have been committed to developing fast and convenient sensing strategies for ATP detection.

Up to date, a variety of ATP detection methods have been reported, including chromatography[7], electrochemical[8], fluorescence[9,10] and chemiluminescence methods[11]. These methods display high sensitivity and selectivity, while there are still a number of challenges when those common methods are used. For example, the reported electrochemical, fluorescence or chemiluminescence methods are mostly using aptamers to recognize ATP[12], it is necessary to couple fluorophores or electroactive species with the aptamer, or modify nanomaterial with aptamers[13]. The modification process is complex, time-consuming and the reagents used are expensive. Chromatographic separation takes a long time, and requires advanced instruments and skilled operators, resulting in higher detection costs. Hence, it is still urgently desired to establish simple, cost-efficient, rapid, label-free and aptamer-free ATP detection methods.

The target-induced inhibition of the catalytic activity of nanozymes has been a popular strategy for developing biosensing[14–16]. Although analyte has been successfully detected with this strategy, the use of additional metal ions and aptamers in the process is still unavoidable. Metal ions or aptamers which were bound to nanozymes reduced the catalytic activity of nanozymes. Subsequently, the catalytic activity of nanozymes recovered due to a stronger affinity between analyte and metal ions or aptamers[17,18].

Carbon dots (CDs) have become one of the most crucial research fields in material fabrication owning to their uniquely fluorescent properties in terms of chemical stability, outstanding water solubility, low cytotoxicity, anti-photobleaching and excellent biocompatibility[19–21]. Based on these excellent performances, great progress has been made in many applications, such as drug delivery[22], bioimaging[23], sensing[24]. In addition, CDs have been reported to detect ATP due to the above fluorescence properties[25–28].

In this work, we have successfully constructed a cost-efficient, simple, and sensitive colorimetric strategy for quantitative detection of ATP. As shown in Scheme 1 , the synthesized CDs reveal a peroxidase-like activity[29], which can catalyze the oxidation of chromogenic substrate 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to generate a blue colored product oxTMB in the presence of H2O2. Interestingly, the introduction of ATP dramatically prevents this catalytic reaction, contributing to a colorless solution. A slight change of ATP content can significantly influence the change of the color of reaction catalyzed by CDs. According tosuch findings, the relationship between the color change of oxTMB and the amount of ATP was investigated, and a highly sensitive method for detecting ATP was established.
Details of the materials and instrument used in this work were presented in the Supplementary information.
The CDs were prepared following our previous report[30], and the details were presented in the Supplementary information.

To detect ATP, CDs solution (1.7 mg/L), ATP (various concentrations), TMB (0.80 mM) and H2O2 (5.0 mM) were mixed with HAc-NaAc buffer (40 mM, pH 4.0), and incubated at 60 ℃ in a water bath for 30 min. Then, the absorption spectra ranging 500–800 nm were measured.

For detecting ATP in fetal calf serum (FBS), 1% FBS was diluted 100-fold with ultrapure water. Then, different concentrations of ATP (0.60, 0.80, 1.0, 1.5 μM) were added to the diluted FBS sample in the presence of CDs solution (1.7 mg/L), TMB (0.80 mM), H2O2 (5.0 mM) and HAc-NaAc buffer (40 mM, pH 4.0). Then, the volume of the chromogenic system was adjusted to 1.0 mL with water. Finally, the absorbance at 652 nm was measured and analyzed after incubating at 60 ℃ for 30 min.

The production of hydroxyl radical was detected by TA. CDs solution (1.7 mg/L), TA (0.80 mM), H2O2 (5.0 mM) and ATP (0, 0.50, 1.0 μM) were mixed with HAc-NaAc buffer (40 mM, pH = 4.0) and maintained at room temperature for 30 min. Then the fluorescence intensity at 450 nm was recorded. The generated superoxide anion was detected by a commercial Total Superoxide Dismutase Assay Kit.

The inhibition mechanism of ATP on CDs catalytic activity was further confirmed by electron spin resonance. CDs solution (1.7 mg/L), acetate buffer (40 mM, pH = 4.0), H2O2 (5.0 mM), ATP (0, 0.50, 1.0 μM), 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrolin-N-oxide (DMPO, 50 mM), which was used to form the DMPO/•OH spin adduct and proper amount of ultrapure water were added into a plastic tube, and the ESR spectra were obtained.

CDs were successfully synthesized by the nitric acid reflux method[30]. The morphology of CDs was investigated by TEM and the results were demonstrated in Fig. 1 a, which displayed that the CDs were evenly and uniformly distributed. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image displayed the high crystallinity of the CDs (Fig. 1b), and the lattice spacing of 0.21 nm was in line with that of the (100) plane of graphite[31]. The average diameter was calculated to be 2.5 ± 0.5 nm (determined from 100 counts, Fig. 1c). The FT-IR spectrum of CDs showed absorption peaks at 1600 cm−1 and 3400 cm−1 (Fig. 1d), and the peaks corresponding to the C = O stretching vibration and O-H chemical bond. These results were consistent with literature and proved the successful synthesis of CDs[32,33].

ce:section.ce:section-title: ATP inhibiting the peroxidase-like activity of CDs.
ce:section.ce:para: As reported by our group[29], CDs possessed peroxidase-like activity. In the presence of H2O2, TMB can be oxidized to produce the oxTMB which exhibits a clear absorption peak at 652 nm. First, as demonstrated in Fig. 2 a, the mixed solution of TMB and H2O2 was colorless and showed a small absorption peak at 652 nm (curve “1″). With the addition of CDs, the colorless solution turned blue, and the absorbance at 652 nm increased significantly, indicating that CDs exhibited inherent peroxidase-like activity (curve “2”). Under the optimal conditions (Figure S1), H2O2 and TMB were selected as substrates to investigate the catalytic activity of CDs (Figure S2 and Table S1) which confirmed that the CDs had a high peroxide mimetic enzyme activity. After adding ATP to the CDs-TMB-H2O2 chromogenic system, the absorption dropped significantly and the blue color of the solution faded (curve “3”), revealing the catalytic activity of CDs can be inhibited by ATP. As shown in “4” of the curve, when ATP was added into TMB-H2O2 mixed solution, the absorbance decreased slightly, indicating that ATP itself had a weak inhibitory effect on the TMB-H2O2 color system. Fig. 2b exhibited that the degree of inhibition increases with the increase of ATP concentration, which proved that ATP can be detected through inhibits the CDs-TMB-H2O2 chromogenic system.

To improve the sensitivity of ATP detection, many important reaction conditions including pH, reaction temperature, H2O2 concentration and incubation time were optimized. ΔA (ΔA = A0 − A) was used as the criterion for optimizing the conditions. A0 and A represent the absorbance of the CDs-TMB-H2O2 chromogenic system at 652 nm without and with ATP, respectively. Fig. 3 a clearly showed that the optimal pH was 4.0. The inhibition curve with temperature variation was shown in Fig. 3b. ΔA increased and reached peak at 60 °C during the temperature increased from 20 °C to 60 °C, while ΔA gradually decreased when the temperature continues to rise above 60 °C. Therefore, 60 °C was the optimal temperature for ATP detection. As shown in Fig. 3c, with the increase of H2O2 concentration, ΔA increases. When the amount of H2O2 exceeds 5.0 mM, ΔA basically remains unchanged. Thus, 5.0 mM H2O2 was chosen as the optimal H2O2 concentration. It can be found from Fig. 3d that ΔA reached a peak at 30 min. Thus 30 min was used to detect ATP.

To understand the mechanism of colorimetric detection ATP, at first, we explored whether ATP would scavenge H2O2 or not. Different concentrations of ATP reacted with H2O2 at 60 ℃ for 30 min, and then the H2O2 content of mixed solution was detected by iodometric method[34]. The result displayed that H2O2 concentration has no significant change compared with blank (Figure S3, RSD = 2.0%), so ATP could not scavenge H2O2.

Next, ROS related to CDs-TMB-H2O2 chromogenic system were studied. As shown in Figure S4, the addition of hydroxyl radical (•OH) scavenger (ascorbic acids and thiourea[35,36]) caused a significant decrease in absorbance of CDs-TMB-H2O2 chromogenic system, while the addition of superoxide anion (O2 •−) scavenger (superoxide dismutase[37]) caused a slight decrease in absorbance. Furthermore, a little change of absorbance was observed upon adding 1O2 scavenger (sodium azide and β-carotene[38,39]). The results showed that the production of •OH plays a major role in the oxidation of TMB to display blue outcome and the O2 •− plays a part role.

Next, a series of experiments were carried out to explore the inhibiting effects of ATP on different ROS. The effect of ATP on O2 •− was discussed with Total Superoxide Dismutase Assay Kit and the results were shown in Figure S5. The absorbance at 460 nm had no obvious change with the addition of different concentration of ATP, indicating ATP can’t scavenge O2 •−.

TA, a fluorescent •OH probe[40], was used to investigate the effect of ATP on •OH. In the absence of ATP, a strong fluorescence signal with a maximal emission at 425 nm can be observed in the solution containing CDs and H2O2. However, in the presence of ATP, the fluorescence signal decreased. Furthermore, with the increase of ATP content, the fluorescence intensity gradually decreases (Fig. 4 a). These results indicated that ATP can inhibit the production of •OH catalyzed by CDs.

We further validated the role of ATP by ESR, which was recognized as the most direct and accurate technique for the determination of radicals[41]. DMPO can capture hydroxyl radicals and generate DMPO/•OH spin adducts, with a typical quadruple characteristic ESR signal of 1:2:2:1[42]. Fig. 4b showed that with the increase of ATP concentration, the signal intensity of DMPO/•OH adduct decreased successively, indicating that the amount of •OH decreases due to the presence of ATP. This is consistent with the above results. These results indicate that ATP inhibits the CDs-TMB-H2O2 chromogenic system by preventing the generation of •OH.

The interaction between CDs and ATP was also explored. Figure S6a showed that ATP had no effect on the fluorescence of CDs, indicating that there was no interaction between CDs and ATP. Next, the absorption spectra of CDs or ATP interacting with substrate were studied (Figure S6b, S6c). Due to the additivity of absorbance[43], the theoretical absorbance of mixed solutions was compared with the measured absorbance. The results displayed that the actual absorbance was lower than the theoretical absorbance indicating ATP or CDs interacting with TMB. The zeta potentials (Figure S6d) of CDs, CDs-ATP, CDs-TMB and CDs-TMB-ATP were measured. There is no significant difference in zeta potentials of CDs-ATP and CDs (P = 0.95), which is consistent with the above fluorescence results, further indicating ATP had no effect on CDs. The stronger positive charge of CDs-TMB than CDs indicates the electrostatic binding between CDs and TMB. Thus, the presence of ATP may affect the binding of CDs to substrates, and the zeta potentials of CDs-TMB-ATP has proved it. Qu’s group has reported that nanozymes exist substrate-binding sites during the catalytic process[44]. Therefore, we hypothesized that the inhibiting effect of ATP on catalytic activity of CDs partly originates from the fact that ATP competes with CDs for TMB. CDs cannot combine with the TMB which is an electronic medium during the catalytic process[45,46], affecting CDs to catalyze TMB to form oxTMB. In summary, ATP not only prevented the formation of •OH, but also affected the interaction between ATP and the chromogenic substrate, thus ATP can be colorimetric detected by inhibiting CDs-TMB-H2O2 chromogenic system.

CDs can catalyze oxidation of TMB by H2O2 to produce blue oxTMB. When ATP was introduced to the solution, it can inhibit the peroxidase-like activity of CDs, resulting in drop in absorbance and discoloration of the solution. A colorimetric method for ATP detection was established based on above found. The decrease of absorbance was related to the concentration of ATP (Fig. 5 a). As demonstrated in Fig. 5b, the decrease of absorption peak at 652 nm was positively correlated with the concentration of ATP. ΔA was proportional to the logarithmic of ATP concentration ranging between 0.050 and 2.0 μM. The regression equation was ΔA = 0.8578 lg[ATP] + 1.128(R2 = 0.9878). Here ΔA = A0 − A, where A0 and A were defined as the absorbance at 652 nm in the absence and presence of ATP. The detection limit (LOD) of ATP concentration was calculated to be 34 nM (S/N = 3, n = 11). The corresponding visualization picture was shown in Fig. 5c. This method exhibited a wide linear range and can be used to detect ATP with naked eyes. We summarized some ATP detection methods in Table S2. Compared with other reported methods, the proposed ATP colorimetric platform was sensitive and has a wide linear range. In addition, this method avoids using expensive instruments.

Nucleotides include GTP, UTP, CTP, ADP, GDP and AMP, amino acids such as L-cysteine (L-Cys), threonine (Thr), tryptophan (Trp), isoleucine (Ire), glycine (Gly), phenylalanine (Phe) and lysine (Lys) were used as potential interferents to test the selectivity of ATP detection. As shown in Fig. 6 a, 0.50 μM ATP caused a dramatic decrease in absorbance, while a relatively slight change of the absorbance was observed in the presence of interfering substances (0.10 μM for GTP, UTP, CTP, 0.50 μM for ADP, GDP, AMP and 5.0 μM for amino acids). Extracellular ATP levels exceed other nucleotides levels by several folds[47], these data indicate that other nucleotides will not interfere with extracellular detection of ATP. Furthermore, we also explored the impact of coexisting substances on the detection of ATP. All the coexisting nucleotides and amino acids had no effect on the detection of 0.50 μM ATP (Fig. 6b). Therefore, this colorimetric method has pleasurable selectivity and can be used for the detection of actual samples.

In order to evaluate the practicability and feasibility of this colorimetric method, ATP in 100-fold diluted 1 %FBS samples with a standard addition method was determined. From Table 1 , the recoveries of ATP in the samples ranged from 90.7% to 102.3% with RSDs less than 5.0%. The results showed that the colorimetric detection method is reliable for the determination of ATP content in complex biological fluids.

In conclusion, we constructed a convenient, simple and cost-effective new strategy for ATP detection based on the fact that ATP inhibits the peroxidase activity of CDs. The role of ATP during CDs catalyzed TMB oxidation was explored in detail. ESR revealed that ATP inhibits the peroxidase-like activity of CDs via preventing the generation of •OH, and zeta potentials displayed that ATP competes with CDs for TMB. Under the optimum conditions, the dynamic range of this colorimetric detection method for ATP was 0.050–2.0 μM and the LOD was 34 nM. What’s more, this assay was successfully used in the diluted FBS sample detection. Utilizing its outstanding detection performance, we imagine that this colorimetric, aptamer-free, and inhibitive peroxidase-like activity CDs-based strategy will provide a more direct, convenient alternative for actual ATP detection.

Na Huang: Conceptualization, Visualization, Methodology, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Jiahui Wen: Software, Investigation. Danyang Yi: Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Zixuan Wei: Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Yijuan Long: Supervision, Resources. Huzhi Zheng: Supervision, Project administration, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (XDJK2020C033).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2021.120658.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
